user_goals_summary: Writes reported, scene-driven longform for a general magazine audience. Prioritizes letting sources speak in their own words over authorial paraphrase, uses neutral attribution, and trusts the reader to interpret quotes and scenes without editorial guidance. Values structural rhythm between intimate individual experience and systemic context.

rubric.name: Source Voice Primacy
rubric.description: Sources speak in their own words early and often. The writer's voice holds space for direct quotes rather than substituting paraphrase for them. Extended passages should not summarize sources' experiences when tape exists.

dimensions.label: Key sources speak in direct quotes within the opening section rather than being described or paraphrased
No extended stretch paraphrases sources' experiences on their behalf when their own words could carry the passage
Direct quotes carry substantive narrative weight — they do real work in the piece, not decoration
Quoted material includes specific, physical details that could only come from someone who was actually there

rubric.name: Clean Attribution
rubric.description: Attribution uses 'said' and gets out of the way. No editorializing through verb choice, adverbs, or framing phrases that tell the reader how to hear a quote. Quotes land without being over-introduced.

dimensions.label: Attribution defaults to 'said' rather than editorializing verbs (emphasized, insisted, explained, described)
No adverbs or characterizing phrases frame how the quote should be received (plainly, quietly, forcefully)
No phrases that remind the reader the piece is a reconstruction (recalled, looking back, recounting the moment)
Quotes are not over-introduced with throat-clearing or excessive setup — they land directly

rubric.name: Authorial Restraint
rubric.description: The writer's voice provides connective tissue and context but never substitutes for sources or tells the reader how to interpret what they're hearing. The writing trusts the reader to draw conclusions from evidence and quotes without editorial guidance.

dimensions.label: Authorial voice provides transitions and context but does not stand in for sources' experiences
The writer does not tell the reader how to feel about a scene or how to hear a quote
The writing trusts the reader to interpret strong material without signposting its significance
Any authorial framing or commentary earns its place by doing work the quotes and scenes cannot do on their own

rubric.name: Scene-Level Immediacy
rubric.description: The piece opens in a specific, grounded moment anchored to a specific person rather than starting with abstraction or context. Scenes are built from concrete sensory details particular enough to feel observed, not summarized.

dimensions.label: The piece opens in a specific, located moment rather than with background, thesis, or abstraction
Scenes are anchored to a named individual's experience, not generalized or anonymized
Scene-setting includes concrete sensory details — temperature, sound, physical space, bodily experience
Details are particular enough to feel witnessed or reported, not compressed into summary

rubric.name: Structural Rhythm
rubric.description: The piece breathes between intimate individual experience and broader systemic context. Transitions between registers feel earned and natural, and contextual information is placed where it serves narrative momentum rather than interrupting it.

dimensions.label: The piece moves between close individual experience and wider systemic or structural context
Shifts between registers feel organic rather than abrupt or mechanical
Background and contextual information is placed where it serves the story's momentum, not where it interrupts it

rubric.name: Narrative Pacing
rubric.description: Information is revealed in a controlled sequence that builds the story forward. The piece sets up what's coming without tipping too far ahead or front-loading conclusions the reader hasn't earned yet.

dimensions.label: Information is sequenced to build forward momentum rather than front-loaded or delivered out of order
Future developments are set up without giving away the story before the reader has lived through it
Each paragraph or section earns its place by advancing the story or deepening understanding

coaching_notes: This user's core principle is that the writer should be invisible infrastructure — holding space for sources rather than standing between them and the reader. Every edit in the conversation moved toward less authorial intervention and more source presence. When in doubt, cut the writer's framing and let the quote land. Attribution is a non-negotiable: 'said' only, no characterizing verbs or adverbs, no metajournalistic distancing. The user has a sharp eye for the same pattern recurring at different scales (editorializing through paraphrase, through attribution verbs, through interpretive framing) and will catch it every time.

rubric.name: Scene-Level Immediacy
rubric.description: The piece opens in a specific, grounded moment anchored to a specific person rather than starting with abstraction or context. Scenes are built from concrete sensory details particular enough to feel observed, not summarized.

rubric.name: Structural Rhythm and Tethering
rubric.description: The piece breathes between intimate individual experience and broader systemic context, but zoom-outs always happen from the character's world, not away from it. Contextual passages stay tethered to the central person — like a rubber band that can stretch toward the systemic but snaps back before the section closes. Sections end on specificity and a person, not on data or abstraction.

dimensions.label: The piece moves between close individual experience and wider systemic or structural context
Contextual and systemic passages remain connected to the central character — the zoom-out happens from their world, not away from it
No more than two consecutive beats of pure system, data, or abstraction without a human reference point
Sections and paragraphs close on concrete, individual detail rather than data, summary, or abstraction
Shifts between registers feel organic and earned rather than abrupt or mechanical

rubric.description: Sources speak in their own words early and often. The writer's voice holds space for direct quotes rather than substituting paraphrase for them. Extended passages should not summarize sources' experiences when tape exists.

rubric.name: Authorial Restraint
rubric.description: The writer's voice provides connective tissue and context but never substitutes for sources, tells the reader how to interpret material, or reaches for rhetorically polished kickers that are really editorial commentary disguised as prose style. The writing trusts the scene to make the point.

dimensions.label: Authorial voice provides transitions and context but does not stand in for sources' experiences
The writer does not tell the reader how to feel about a scene or how to hear a quote
The writing trusts the reader to interpret strong material without signposting its significance
The prose does not reach for neat, rhetorically balanced closing lines that are really the writer making a point rather than the story making it

rubric.name: Clean Attribution
rubric.description: Attribution uses 'said' and gets out of the way. No editorializing through verb choice, adverbs, or framing phrases that tell the reader how to hear a quote. Quotes land without being over-introduced.

dimensions.label: Attribution defaults to 'said' rather than editorializing verbs (emphasized, insisted, explained, described)
No adverbs or characterizing phrases frame how the quote should be received (plainly, quietly, forcefully)
No phrases that remind the reader the piece is a reconstruction (recalled, looking back, recounting the moment)
Quotes are not over-introduced with throat-clearing or excessive setup — they land directly

coaching_notes: This user's dominant craft instinct is toward restraint — letting sources, scenes, and details do the work rather than authorial voice. Their most persistent concern across both iterations is structural: specifically, that contextual or systemic passages must remain tethered to the central character rather than drifting into briefing mode. They will iterate extensively on sequencing and rhythm within a single paragraph to get this right. When the user says something 'pulls them out,' it almost always means the writing has reminded them of the writer's presence — through editorial commentary, characterizing attribution, or meta-journalistic framing. The fix is nearly always subtraction or reordering, not addition.

user_goals_summary: Writes reported, character-driven longform pieces for general-audience magazines. rubric.name: Scene-Level Immediacy
rubric.description: The piece opens in a specific, grounded moment anchored to a specific person rather than starting with abstraction or context. Scenes are built from concrete details — sensory, material, economic — particular enough to feel observed, not summarized.

dimensions.label: The piece opens in a specific, located moment rather than with background, thesis, or abstraction
Scenes are anchored to a named individual's experience, not generalized or anonymized

Details are particular enough to feel witnessed or reported, not compressed into summary

rubric.name: Structural Rhythm and Tethering
rubric.description: The piece breathes between intimate individual experience and broader systemic context, but zoom-outs always happen from the character's world, not away from it, and land on concrete factual ground, not vague gestures at scale. Like a rubber band that stretches toward the systemic but snaps back before the section closes. Sections end on specificity and a person, not on data or abstraction.

Sections and paragraphs close on concrete, individual detail rather than data, summary, or abstraction
Shifts between registers feel organic and earned — like the piece exhaling — rather than abrupt, mechanical, or like a rescue from abstraction

rubric.name: Authorial Restraint
rubric.description: The writer's voice provides connective tissue and context but never substitutes for sources, tells the reader how to interpret material, or reaches for rhetorically polished kickers that are really editorial commentary disguised as prose style. The writing trusts the scene to make the point. Brief, earned reframing is acceptable; sustained authorial commentary is not.

dimensions.label: Attribution defaults to 'said' rather than editorializing verbs (emphasized, insisted, explained, described)

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: source voices in their own words early, 'said' for attribution with no editorializing verbs or framing, and zoom-outs that stay tethered to the central character and land on concrete factual ground. This user diagnoses problems precisely and prefers reacting to concrete options over answering abstract questions — offer 2-3 specific alternatives rather than asking what they want. They are highly sensitive to any moment that creates distance between the reader and the material, whether through metajournalistic flags, authorial commentary, or vague gestures at scale that substitute atmosphere for substance.

rubric.name: Scene-Level Immediacy
rubric.description: The piece opens in a specific, grounded moment anchored to a specific person rather than starting with abstraction or context. Scenes are built from concrete details — sensory, material, economic — particular enough to feel observed, not summarized.

rubric.name: Structural Rhythm and Tethering
rubric.description: The piece breathes between intimate individual experience and broader systemic context, but zoom-outs happen from the character's world, not away from it. Like a rubber band: stretches toward the systemic but snaps back. Systemic passages should feel like one continuous movement with the character inside them — their physical or material reality embedded within data and policy sentences, not bolted on before or after. Data flows out of the character's specific situation rather than arriving independently.

dimensions.label: The piece moves between close individual experience and wider systemic or structural context
Contextual passages stay tethered to the central character and land on specific, concrete ground — named systems, real data, identifiable actors — rather than vague evocative gestures at scale

The character's physical or material reality appears inside systemic sentences — as an offhand detail, not a spotlight — so data and policy feel measured against a specific body or situation rather than floating above the story
Systemic and individual registers feel like one continuous movement rather than two zones with a visible seam between them
Sections and paragraphs close on concrete, individual detail rather than data, summary, or abstraction

rubric.name: Source Voice Primacy
rubric.description: Sources speak in their own words early and often. The writer's voice holds space for direct quotes rather than substituting paraphrase for them. Quotes carry substantive weight — they do work the authorial voice cannot — and each quote takes the reader somewhere the surrounding prose hasn't already gone. A quote should extend, not confirm.

dimensions.label: Key sources speak in direct quotes within the opening section rather than being described or paraphrased
No extended stretch paraphrases sources' experiences on their behalf when their own words could carry the passage
Direct quotes carry substantive narrative weight — they do real work in the piece, not decoration
Each quote takes the reader somewhere the surrounding prose hasn't already gone — it extends understanding rather than confirming what the reader already felt
Quoted material includes specific, physical details that could only come from someone who was actually there

rubric.name: Authorial Restraint
rubric.description: The writer's voice provides connective tissue and context but never substitutes for sources, tells the reader how to interpret material, or reaches for rhetorically polished kickers that are editorial commentary disguised as prose style. Friction and tension are created through juxtaposition and sensory detail, not named analytically. The writing trusts the scene to make the point. Closing lines and quotes are unresolved and raw, not tidy or philosophical.

dimensions.label: Authorial voice provides transitions and context but does not stand in for sources' experiences
The writer does not tell the reader how to feel about a scene or how to hear a quote
The prose does not reach for neat, rhetorically balanced closing lines that are really the writer making a point rather than the story making it
Tension between individual experience and systemic forces is created through sensory juxtaposition, not stated analytically — the reader feels the friction rather than being told it exists
Closing beats feel raw and still-inside-it rather than composed, philosophical, or arrived-at — the reader is left in the waiting, not handed a summary of what it meant

rubric.name: Narrative Pacing and Weight
rubric.description: Information is revealed in a controlled sequence that builds the story forward. The piece earns each transition rather than front-loading conclusions. Duration and waiting are rendered as texture — specific, lived, physical — rather than as chronology or elapsed time. When the piece needs stillness, it stays on one image long enough for the reader to feel it.

dimensions.label: Information is sequenced to build forward momentum rather than front-loaded or delivered out of order
Future developments are set up without giving away the story before the reader has lived through it
The passage slows down and lands on one image or detail when it needs weight — duration feels like duration, not a timeline of events
Each paragraph or section earns its place by advancing the story or deepening understanding rather than restating or padding

rubric.name: Clean Attribution
rubric.description: Attribution uses 'said' and gets out of the way. No editorializing through verb choice, adverbs, or framing phrases that tell the reader how to hear a quote or imply something about the speaker's stance. Quotes land without being over-introduced, flagged as reconstructions, or framed with characterizing language. Physical details accompanying quotes float free of attribution verbs when possible.

dimensions.label: Attribution defaults to 'said' rather than editorializing verbs (emphasized, insisted, explained, described) that imply something about the speaker's intent or stance
No adverbs or characterizing phrases frame how the quote should be received (plainly, quietly, forcefully)
No phrases that remind the reader the piece is a reconstruction (recalled, looking back, recounting the moment, said later)
Quotes are not over-introduced with throat-clearing or excessive setup — they land directly

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: sources must speak in their own words early; 'said' is the only acceptable default attribution verb; no editorial commentary disguised as prose rhythm. Core trade-off the user consistently makes: will sacrifice explanatory clarity or completeness to keep the central character physically present in every passage — if systemic context can't be delivered through or around the character, it should be cut or compressed. The user's revision instinct is almost always subtractive — toward leaner, quieter, more offhand prose — and they respond best when the assistant identifies the exact sentence or clause causing the problem rather than proposing wholesale rewrites.

rubric.name: Scene-Level Immediacy
rubric.description: The piece opens in a specific, grounded moment anchored to a specific person rather than starting with abstraction or context. Scenes are built from concrete details — sensory, material, economic — particular enough to feel observed, not summarized.

dimensions.label: The piece opens in a specific, located moment rather than with background, thesis, or abstraction
Scenes are anchored to a named individual's experience, not generalized or anonymized
Scene-setting includes concrete sensory details — temperature, sound, physical space, bodily experience
Scenes include specific material or economic details — dollar amounts, logistical calculations, the practical math of a constrained life — that make abstract issues feel lived
Details are particular enough to feel witnessed or reported, not compressed into summary

rubric.name: Source Voice Primacy
rubric.description: Sources speak in their own words early and often. The writer's voice holds space for direct quotes rather than substituting paraphrase for them. Quotes carry substantive weight — they do work the authorial voice cannot — and each quote takes the reader somewhere the surrounding prose hasn't already gone. A quote should extend, not confirm.

dimensions.label: Key sources speak in direct quotes within the opening section rather than being described or paraphrased
No extended stretch paraphrases sources' experiences on their behalf when their own words could carry the passage
Direct quotes carry substantive narrative weight — they do real work in the piece, not decoration
Each quote takes the reader somewhere the surrounding prose hasn't already gone — it extends understanding rather than confirming what the reader already felt
Quoted material includes specific, physical details that could only come from someone who was actually there

rubric.name: Structural Rhythm and Tethering
rubric.description: The piece breathes between intimate individual experience and broader systemic context, but zoom-outs happen from the character's world, not away from it. Like a rubber band: stretches toward the systemic but snaps back. Systemic passages should feel like one continuous movement with the character inside them — their physical or material reality embedded within data and policy sentences, not bolted on before or after. Data flows out of the character's specific situation rather than arriving independently.

dimensions.label: The piece moves between close individual experience and wider systemic or structural context
Contextual passages stay tethered to the central character and land on specific, concrete ground — named systems, real data, identifiable actors — rather than vague evocative gestures at scale

The character's physical or material reality appears inside systemic sentences — as an offhand detail, not a spotlight — so data and policy feel measured against a specific body or situation rather than floating above the story
Systemic and individual registers feel like one continuous movement rather than two zones with a visible seam between them
Sections and paragraphs close on concrete, individual detail rather than data, summary, or abstraction

rubric.name: Authorial Restraint
rubric.description: The writer's voice provides connective tissue and context but never substitutes for sources, tells the reader how to interpret material, or reaches for rhetorically polished kickers that are editorial commentary disguised as prose style. Friction and tension are created through juxtaposition and sensory detail, not named analytically. The writing trusts the scene to make the point. Closing lines and quotes are unresolved and raw, not tidy or philosophical.

dimensions.label: Authorial voice provides transitions and context but does not stand in for sources' experiences
The writer does not tell the reader how to feel about a scene or how to hear a quote
The prose does not reach for neat, rhetorically balanced closing lines that are really the writer making a point rather than the story making it
Tension between individual experience and systemic forces is created through sensory juxtaposition, not stated analytically — the reader feels the friction rather than being told it exists
Closing beats feel raw and still-inside-it rather than composed, philosophical, or arrived-at — the reader is left in the waiting, not handed a summary of what it meant

rubric.name: Clean Attribution
rubric.description: Attribution uses 'said' and gets out of the way. No editorializing through verb choice, adverbs, or framing phrases that tell the reader how to hear a quote or imply something about the speaker's stance. Quotes land without being over-introduced, flagged as reconstructions, or framed with characterizing language. Physical details accompanying quotes float free of attribution verbs when possible.

dimensions.label: Attribution defaults to 'said' rather than editorializing verbs (emphasized, insisted, explained, described) that imply something about the speaker's intent or stance
No adverbs or characterizing phrases frame how the quote should be received (plainly, quietly, forcefully)
No phrases that remind the reader the piece is a reconstruction (recalled, looking back, recounting the moment, said later)
Quotes are not over-introduced with throat-clearing or excessive setup — they land directly

user_goals_summary: The user writes reported longform features for general-audience magazines, centering workers' lived experiences within systemic stories. They value prose that trusts its material — opening in specific grounded moments, letting sources speak in their own words, keeping the central character physically present even during policy or data passages, and closing on unresolved human specificity rather than analytical summary. The writer's voice should hold space for the reporting, never substitute for it.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: sources must speak in their own words early; 'said' is the only acceptable default attribution verb; no editorial commentary disguised as prose rhythm. Core trade-off the user consistently makes: will sacrifice explanatory clarity or completeness to keep the central character physically present in every passage — if systemic context can't be delivered through or around the character, it should be cut or compressed. The user's revision instinct is almost always subtractive — toward leaner, quieter, more offhand prose — and they respond best when the assistant identifies the exact sentence or clause causing the problem rather than proposing wholesale rewrites.
